description: README.md is significantly out of date. Says six tutorial chapters complete (there are 13), roadmap tables show kernel primitives and fc.py features as not-started when most are done, references getting-started/ directory which is actually docs/, and the status section doesn't reflect current project state.

The primitives table stops at the early set (DUP, DROP, SWAP, OVER, +, -, @, !, EMIT, CR, HALT). Missing all primitives added for chapters 6-12: DO, LOOP, I, *, /MOD, KEY, 0BRANCH, BRANCH, =, <>, <, >, 0=, AT. Memory layout also shows an old START address.

summary: forth/tools/README.md shows completed features as not-yet
description: The 'What fc.py does not do' table still lists IF/THEN/LOOP as not yet implemented — they are done. The compilation pipeline description omits DO/LOOP, IF/ELSE/THEN, BEGIN/AGAIN/UNTIL. Uses CLOADM which should be LOADM.

summary: Bare Naked kernel: replace POLCAT with direct PIA0 keyboard scan
description: CODE_KEY (line 350 of forth/kernel/kernel.asm) is the kernel's only ROM dependency, calling JSR [$A000] (POLCAT via Extended Color BASIC ROM). Replacing this with direct PIA0 access makes the kernel fully ROM-independent and ready for cartridge delivery without BASIC loaded. Work includes: (1) new KBD-SCAN primitive writing col_mask to PIA0-B ($FF02) and reading PIA0-A ($FF00); (2) rewritten CODE_KEY with register-based debounce and MATRIX2ASCII lookup table; (3) AND and OR primitives; (4) KEY-HELD? Forth word; (5) optional KEY? non-blocking variant. Also requires fc.py word table updates for kbd-scan, and, or.

Add CODE_AND ( n1 n2 -- n ) and CODE_OR ( n1 n2 -- n ) to kernel.asm (CFA table entries + implementations). Add 'and' and 'or' to fc.py kernel_words dict. These are needed by KEY-HELD? and general bitwise application code.

summary: PIA0-A direction register init in START
description: Add bare-metal PIA0 initialisation to the START routine in kernel.asm: write to PIA0-A control register ($FF01) to set port A as input and enable the data register, and write to PIA0-B control register ($FF03) to set port B as output. BASIC does this at boot; a bare-metal cartridge must do it explicitly. Prerequisite for KBD-SCAN and the new CODE_KEY.

Add CODE_KBD_SCAN to kernel.asm: write col_mask to PIA0-B ($FF02), read PIA0-A ($FF00), invert, mask bit 7 (joystick comparator), push result. Passing $00 strobes all columns simultaneously for a fast any-key check. Add 'kbd-scan' to fc.py kernel_words. Depends on issue 6 (PIA0 init).

Replace CODE_KEY's JSR [$A000] / POLCAT call with a direct PIA0 scan loop. Includes: (1) register-based two-poll debounce (no RAM needed); (2) MATRIX2ASCII BSR subroutine with a 56-entry lookup table (7 cols x 8 rows) mapping matrix position to ASCII; (3) conditional assembly (IFDEF COCO3) for CoCo 3 variant table covering F1/F2/ALT. Eliminates the last ROM dependency. Depends on issues 6 and 7.

Implement INCLUDE <filename> as a compile-time host directive in fc.py. When the tokeniser encounters INCLUDE, it reads the named file (relative to the source file's directory) and splices its tokens into the stream before continuing. Enables a lib/ directory of shared Forth source. Standard ANS Forth FILE word set behaviour, implemented as a cross-compiler host operation.

summary: KEY-HELD? word and KB-Cx constants in lib/keyboard.fs
description: Create forth/lib/keyboard.fs containing: column mask constants (KB-C0 through KB-C6) as Forth CONSTANT definitions, and KEY-HELD? ( col_mask row_bit -- flag ) defined in pure Forth as: SWAP KBD-SCAN AND. Depends on issues 5 (AND), 7 (KBD-SCAN), and 9 (INCLUDE). Applications include it with: INCLUDE lib/keyboard.fs

Add CODE_KEY_NB to kernel.asm: same PIA0 scan as CODE_KEY but returns 0 immediately if no key is pressed rather than spinning. Useful for game loops, timeouts, and polling. Add 'key?' to fc.py kernel_words. Depends on issue 8 (MATRIX2ASCII table, which KEY? will share via BSR).

Create src/typewriter/typewriter.fs: a simple app that calls KEY in a loop and EMITs each character to the screen. Tests the new PIA0-based KEY implementation end-to-end. ENTER advances to the next row (CR), CLEAR resets cursor to top-left. Includes cursor display, arrow key movement, backspace, SHIFT support, and VSYNC-based auto-repeat.

Classic snake game using VDG semigraphics-4 color blocks. Arrow keys control direction, snake grows when eating food, game over on wall/self collision. Exercises keyboard input, game loop timing (VSYNC), screen drawing, and game state management.

Arrow keys move a brush cursor, number keys select colors. Uses VDG semigraphics modes for a 64x32 color pixel canvas. Demonstrates semigraphics block characters, color selection, and interactive screen painting.
Explore the CoCo's 6-bit DAC on PIA1. Simple tone generator or melody player. Could play a sequence of notes or allow interactive keyboard-to-tone mapping (piano). Opens up PIA1 hardware access.
Cellular automaton on the VDG semigraphics grid. Visually satisfying and a good stress test for Forth performance on the 6809. Could use semigraphics-4 for color or text mode for simplicity.
Stars flying toward the viewer, simulating depth with different speeds. Pure VDG semigraphics, no math library needed. VSYNC-timed animation loop.
Big digit display using block characters, ticks off the VSYNC 60Hz counter. Could include a stopwatch mode with start/stop/reset. Demonstrates timed display updates and large character rendering.

Random recursive backtracker draws maze walls in real time using semigraphics, then lets the player navigate with arrow keys. Requires a random number generator (could seed from VSYNC timing or keyboard).

Navigate between rooms with N/S/E/W commands. Room descriptions, items to pick up, simple puzzles. Pushes the Forth toolchain into string handling, data tables for room connections, and state tracking. A CoCo classic.
Walking character or bouncing ball using VDG semigraphics. Explores smooth movement, erase-draw cycles, and VSYNC-timed animation on the CoCo's character-mapped display.

Hex dump display, live memory editor, peek/poke any address. Arrow keys navigate, hex input to modify bytes. Actually useful as a development tool for inspecting kernel state, variables, and screen RAM.
Wait for a random delay, flash the screen, measure how fast the player hits a key. Uses VSYNC frame counter for timing. Simple but fun, and demonstrates precise timing measurement.

Hook up the CoCo's serial port (PIA1 bit-banged or cartridge slot UART) and make a dumb terminal. Most ambitious hardware-wise — connects the CoCo to the outside world. Practical for DriveWire or other serial communication.

Disassemble memory regions into 6809 mnemonics. Takes a start address and length, displays address, hex bytes, and decoded instruction. Must handle variable-length instructions (1-5 bytes), $10/$11 prefix pages, and indexed addressing postbyte decoding. Big but highly useful for inspecting compiled Forth output and kernel code on-device.

Display data stack and return stack contents on screen. Show stack depth and values in hex. Small enough to be a library word — no kernel changes needed. Immediately useful for debugging any Forth program. Could be invoked with a word like .S (print stack).

Show all compiled words with their CFA addresses. Helps verify that fc.py produced what you expected. Could display kernel primitives and app-defined words with their entry points. Requires fc.py to emit a word table or symbol list into the binary.

A Forth word (e.g. BRK or DEBUG) that can be inserted anywhere in Forth code. When executed, dumps registers and stack contents to the screen and waits for a keypress before continuing. Like a printf-debugger for Forth. Implementable as a library word using existing primitives.

Diff two memory regions byte-by-byte, highlighting differences. Takes two addresses and a length. Useful for verifying binaries match expectations or checking if RAM has been corrupted. Display shows address, expected byte, actual byte for each mismatch.

Single-step through the Forth thread, showing each word as it executes with stack state. The most powerful debugging tool but also the hardest — requires hooking DOCOL and NEXT in the kernel to intercept each threaded dispatch. Could show CFA address, word name (if symbol table available), and stack contents at each step.

Library for PMODE hi-res graphics modes. Requires bare-metal SAM ($FFC0-$FFC5) and VDG ($FF22) register configuration to switch between text and graphics modes. Includes: PMODE-SET ( mode -- ) to select resolution/color depth, SCREEN ( type -- ) for color set, PCLS ( color -- ) to clear graphics page, pixel address calculation. Supports PMODE 0-4 (128x96 to 256x192). Prerequisite for LINE, CIRCLE, PAINT, and all hi-res demos.

summary: Lib: Pixel plot and line drawing
description: PSET ( x y color -- ) plots a pixel in the current PMODE graphics page. PRESET ( x y -- ) clears a pixel. LINE ( x1 y1 x2 y2 color -- ) draws a line using Bresenham's algorithm. These are the foundation for all hi-res drawing. Depends on issue 31 (PMODE library).

CIRCLE ( cx cy r color -- ) draws a circle using the midpoint circle algorithm. Optional filled variant. Extended Color BASIC's CIRCLE also supports aspect ratio and arc segments — consider a simplified subset. Depends on issue 32 (pixel/line drawing).

PAINT ( x y fill-color border-color -- ) flood-fills a bounded region. Extended Color BASIC uses a scanline seed fill. On the 6809 with limited RAM, a simple stack-based fill may hit memory limits — consider a scanline approach. Depends on issue 32.

A DRAW-like command inspired by Extended Color BASIC's DRAW string language. Compact command strings like 'U10R5D10L5' to draw shapes. Commands: U/D/L/R (up/down/left/right), E/F/G/H (diagonals), M (move to), B (blank move), N (no cursor update), A (angle 0-3), S (scale), C (color). Extremely efficient for defining complex shapes in minimal code. Implement as a Forth word that interprets a command string stored in memory.

SOUND ( pitch duration -- ) generates a tone via the CoCo's 6-bit DAC on PIA1 ($FF20 bits 2-7). Pitch controls frequency by toggling the DAC output in a timed loop, duration controls length. Also expose DAC-OUT ( value -- ) for direct 6-bit DAC writes (values 0-63 shifted left 2 into $FF20). Foundation for all sound/music features.

A PLAY-like command inspired by Extended Color BASIC's PLAY string language. Compact music notation like 'T5O3CDEFGAB' to play melodies. Commands: A-G (notes), O (octave 1-5), L (note length), T (tempo 1-31), P (pause), # (sharp). Extremely efficient for encoding music in minimal code. Uses the DAC (issue 36) for tone generation. Implement as a Forth word that interprets a music string stored in memory.

summary: Demo: Joy to the World (music + graphics)
description: Inspired by the Extended Color BASIC manual's sample program. Plays 'Joy to the World' melody while drawing colorful graphics on screen. Combines the PLAY music library (issue 37) with PMODE hi-res graphics (issue 31). A showcase of audio-visual capability.

Inspired by the Extended Color BASIC manual's animated rocket program. Uses GET/PUT-style sprite blitting to move a rocket shape across a hi-res graphics screen. Demonstrates sprite animation, erase-draw cycles, and PMODE graphics. Depends on issues 31-32 (graphics library).

Inspired by the Extended Color BASIC manual's custom character program. Renders text characters in PMODE hi-res graphics using pixel-level font definitions. Shows how to display readable text on a graphics screen where the normal VDG text mode is unavailable. Depends on issues 31-32.

Inspired by the Extended Color BASIC manual's Bach Invention program. Plays a classical piece using the PLAY music library. Demonstrates complex musical notation, tempo changes, and sustained performance. A pure audio demo showcasing the DAC sound system. Depends on issue 37 (PLAY library).

Inspired by the Color BASIC manual's electronic piano. Maps keyboard keys to musical tones — press A through K to play notes from middle C up one octave. Visual feedback shows which note is playing. A simple but satisfying interactive demo. Depends on issue 36 (sound library).

Inspired by the Color BASIC manual's kaleidoscope program. Draws symmetric random patterns using semigraphics-4 SET/RESET. Four-way symmetry creates mesmerizing visual effects. Uses random colors and positions with VSYNC timing. A classic CoCo demo that works with existing semigraphics capabilities — no PMODE needed.

Inspired by the Color BASIC manual's Ready Aim Fire game. Move a cannon left/right along the bottom of the screen, fire at targets. Text-mode semigraphics with sound effects. Exercises keyboard input, animation, collision detection, and scoring.

Inspired by the Color BASIC manual's typing test program. Displays a phrase, times the user as they type it, calculates words per minute. Uses VSYNC frame counter for accurate timing. Counts errors by comparing input character-by-character. A practical demo that exercises KEY?, screen output, and timing.

Inspired by the Color BASIC manual's music composer program. Generates random melodies with user-selectable length and tempo. Uses random note selection from a scale, with color screen changes on each note. A generative art/music piece. Depends on issue 36 (sound library).

Inspired by the Color BASIC manual's bouncing dot program. A ball bounces around the screen, reflecting off walls. Uses semigraphics-4 SET/RESET for smooth-looking animation. Could evolve into a simple Pong or Breakout. VSYNC-timed for consistent speed.

Inspired by the Color BASIC manual's dice and craps programs. Simulates rolling two dice with ASCII art dice faces on screen. Implements full craps rules: 7/11 on first roll wins, 2/3/12 loses, otherwise roll for point. Sound effects for rolls and results. Needs a random number generator (VSYNC-seeded).

Inspired by the Color BASIC manual's speed reading program. Displays text one line at a time at a user-selected words-per-minute rate. Could use a stored passage (e.g. opening of a novel). Tests how fast you can read and comprehend. VSYNC-timed display updates.

Inspired by the Color BASIC manual's arithmetic and 'Beat the Computer' programs. Generates random math problems (addition, subtraction, maybe multiplication). Player must answer before a timer runs out. Tracks score and percentage correct. Sound feedback for right/wrong answers. VSYNC timing for countdown display.

Reusable library for controlling the MC6847 VDG and SAM chip display modes. Words: set-sam-v ( v -- ) sets SAM V0-V2 display mode bits; set-sam-f ( offset -- ) sets SAM F0-F6 display offset; set-pia ( bits -- ) sets PIA $FF22 bits 7-3 preserving 2-0; reset-text ( -- ) restores default Alpha text mode. Encapsulates all the fiddly SAM/PIA register poking so graphics programs don't have to.

Reusable 5x7 pixel font for rendering text in graphics modes. 37 glyphs: space, A-Z, 0-9. 7 bytes per glyph with pixels in bits 7-3. Written to $6000 at init using fb pattern from Tetris. glyph-addr ( char -- addr ) maps ASCII to font data address. Reusable by any program needing text in non-Alpha VDG modes.

Cycles through all 11 MC6847 display modes: Alpha Internal, SG4, SG6, CG1-CG6, RG1-RG6. Mode table at $5800: 11 entries x 16 bytes with VRAM base, size, PIA/SAM values, mode name. Text modes write VDG chars directly; RG modes use font byte per row; CG modes nibble-expand font. Each mode shows a visual pattern (color bars, stripes, checkerboard) plus the mode name. Key press advances to next mode; loops forever. Depends on #51 (vdg.fs) and #52 (font5x7.fs).

Matrix-style falling green characters on black VDG text screen. 32 independent columns with random speeds, bright inverse-video heads and dim normal trails. Trail mutation and random spawning create an organic feel. SAM double-speed for fast updates. Uses rng.fs and screen.fs libraries.

Full SG4 semigraphics Tetris. 10x16 board, 7 standard pieces with rotation, gravity with speed-up, line clearing, scoring, next-piece preview, high score table. Zero-flicker draw-first-clean-stale rendering. Arrow keys move/rotate, space for hard drop. Key repeat with DAS timing. Uses rng.fs, screen.fs, print.fs, datawrite.fs libraries.

Pixel primitives for RG6 mode (PMODE 4) with NTSC artifact coloring. Treats pixel pairs as artifact-color units (00=black, 11=white, 10=blue, 01=red) at effective 128x192 resolution. Words: rg-pset ( x y color -- ) plot artifact pixel, rg-pget ( x y -- color ) read pixel, rg-pcls ( -- ) clear to black, rg-hline ( x1 x2 y color -- ) fast horizontal line. VRAM at $0E00, 6144 bytes, 32 bytes/row. Foundation for all Space Warp graphics.

summary: Lib: Bresenham line drawing for RG6 (rg-pixel.fs)
description: Add rg-line ( x1 y1 x2 y2 color -- ) to rg-pixel.fs using Bresenham's algorithm. Draws lines in artifact color for maser beams, enemy fire, and tactical display borders. Must handle all octants correctly. Depends on #56.

Software sprites for RG6 artifact-color mode. Sprite data format: width, height, then row bytes (2bpp artifact-color packed). spr-draw ( addr x y -- ) draws sprite with color 0 as transparent. spr-erase ( addr x y -- ) erases sprite footprint to black. Background save/restore for clean overdraw without flicker. Used for Endever, Jovians, bases, stars, triton missile ball. Depends on #56.

Test application for prototyping RG6 artifact-color graphics before building Space Warp. Exercises pixel plot, line drawing at various angles, sprite draw/erase, and text rendering. Visual verification via XRoar with NTSC artifact display. Depends on #56-58.

summary: Lib: Sine/cosine lookup table for angle-based firing
description: 8-bit sine/cosine lookup table for converting firing angles (0-360 degrees) to dx/dy vectors. 91-entry table (0-90 degrees) with quadrant mirroring. Fixed-point values scaled for tactical view dimensions. Used by masers and triton missiles for angle-to-direction conversion.

summary: Space Warp: galaxy generation (8x8 grid, random placement)
description: Generate the 8x8 galaxy at game start based on difficulty level. Each quadrant byte packed: storm(1) | black_hole(1) | stars(3) | base(1) | jovians(2). Distribute Jovians (8-89 based on level), bases (4-10), stars (0-5 per quadrant), black holes, and magnetic storms. When entering a quadrant, expand to full state with pixel positions for all objects.

summary: Space Warp: tactical view rendering (border, stars, sprites)
description: Render the tactical quadrant view in the upper 128x144 artifact pixels. Draw border, place star/planet dots in random artifact colors, draw sprites for Endever (blue), Jovians (red), bases (blue). Refresh loop redraws objects at their current positions. Depends on #56-58.

summary: Space Warp: status panel rendering (RG6 text)
description: Render the status panel in the lower 128x48 artifact pixels (rows 144-191). Display STARDATE, QUADRANT, ENERGY, DEFLECTORS, MISSILES, CONDITION, SOS alerts, and COMMAND? prompt. Update values each frame. Uses rg-text.fs for white text on black background.

summary: Space Warp: real-time arrow key movement
description: Arrow keys move the Endever within the tactical view using ion engines. Movement is continuous while held and works concurrently with command input. Uses KEY? polling each frame. Movement speed affected by ion engine damage. Consumes small amount of ship energy. Depends on #62.

summary: Space Warp: command input system (number keys + params)
description: Command dispatch: number keys 1-7 trigger commands. Some require additional numeric input (angles 0-360, coordinates 0-7, energy 0-100). Input is entered digit-by-digit while the game loop continues (arrow keys still work, Jovians still fire). Display typed digits in status panel. Depends on #63.

summary: Space Warp: masers (command 5 — angle fire, blue beam)
description: Press 5, enter angle 0-360. Draw blue line from Endever at specified angle across tactical view. Line stops when hitting a Jovian (deal damage based on distance and shield level), a star/black hole (blocked), or screen edge (miss). Maser power reduced by deflector setting and maser damage. Consumes energy. Depends on #57, #60, #65.

summary: Space Warp: triton missiles (command 6 — moving blue ball)
description: Press 6, enter angle 0-360. Animate a blue energy ball moving from Endever along the firing angle. One-hit kill on Jovian contact. Blocked by stars and black holes. Limited supply (start with 10, replenished by docking). Ball visibly travels across screen for dramatic effect. Depends on #58, #60, #65.

summary: Space Warp: long range scan (command 3 — galaxy map)
description: Press 3 to replace tactical view with 8x8 galaxy map. Show E=Endever, B=base, 1-3=Jovian count, M=magnetic storm per quadrant. Column/row headers 0-7. Data reflects last-known state (goes stale as Jovians move). Damaged scanners show garbled/missing data. Press any key to return. Depends on #61, #65.

Press 1 to show full-screen damage report. Display Jovians left, bases left, and operational percentage for all 5 systems (ion engines, hyperdrive, scanners, deflectors, masers). Press any key to return to tactical view. Depends on #65.

summary: Space Warp: hyperdrive (command 2 — warp between quadrants)
description: Press 2, enter 2-digit destination (col 0-7, row 0-7). Warp to new quadrant, arrive at random position. Energy cost proportional to distance. Damaged hyperdrive may fail or mis-jump. Expand new quadrant state and redraw tactical view. Depends on #61, #62, #65.

summary: Space Warp: deflectors (command 4 — shield energy setting)
description: Press 4, enter 0-100 to set shield percentage. Higher shields absorb more damage but reduce maser power (50%=1/3 loss, 100%=2/3 loss). Maximum capped by deflector system damage. Setting persists until changed. Depends on #65.
Press 7, confirm with code 123 + ENTER. Destroys Endever and all Jovians in current quadrant. Game ends with final score. Last resort when surrounded. Depends on #65.

Ship energy percentage depleted by: movement (small), maser fire (moderate), damage taken (variable, reduced by shields). Energy reaches 0% = ship destroyed. Recharged to 100% by docking. Status panel shows current energy. Depends on #63.

summary: Space Warp: ship damage system (5 systems, degradation)
description: Five ship systems each with 0-100% operational level. Damage applied when hit (random system, random severity). Each system degrades gameplay: ion engines slow movement, hyperdrive fails, scanners garble data, deflectors cap shield max, masers weaken. All repaired to 100% by docking. Depends on #73.

summary: Space Warp: Jovian AI (movement, firing, targeting)
description: Jovian behavior thread runs each frame alongside player thread. Jovians: fire red lines at Endever and/or bases, dodge player shots, move within quadrant, move between quadrants to hunt bases, call reinforcements to quadrants with bases at higher difficulty. AI aggression scales with difficulty level. Depends on #62, #66.

summary: Space Warp: docking (repair, resupply at bases)
description: Fly Endever directly above or below a base to dock. Status shows ENDEVER DOCKED, condition changes to DOCKED. Repairs all 5 systems to 100%, restores energy to 100%, replenishes triton missiles to 10. Stardates continue during docking. Depends on #64, #73, #74.

summary: Space Warp: SOS alerts (base under attack warnings)
description: When Jovians in another quadrant are attacking a base, flash SOS-BASE x y in the status panel showing the base's quadrant coordinates. Alert persists until base is destroyed or Jovians are cleared. Urgency indicator for the player to warp and defend. Depends on #75.

summary: Space Warp: black holes (invisible hazard, shot blocking)
description: Black holes occupy positions in quadrants but are invisible on the tactical display. Maser beams and triton missiles that hit a black hole disappear silently. Flying the Endever into a black hole destroys it instantly. Player must deduce positions from blocked shots. Depends on #62, #66, #67.

Magnetic storms block scanner readings in affected quadrants. Long range scan shows M instead of contents. Player must warp into a storm quadrant to discover what's there. May also interfere with hyperdrive navigation. Depends on #68.

summary: Space Warp: scoring and difficulty levels
description: 10 difficulty levels controlling Jovian fleet size (8-89), base count, and AI aggression. Score 1-250 based on: fraction of Jovians destroyed, fraction of bases surviving, stardates elapsed, difficulty level. Score calculated at game end. Depends on #73.

summary: Space Warp: win/lose conditions and game over screen
description: Win: all Jovians destroyed + at least 1 base survives. Lose: all bases destroyed, Endever destroyed (0% energy or black hole), or self-destruct. Display final score and CARE TO PLAY AGAIN? prompt. Restart on keypress. Depends on #80.

summary: Space Warp: title screen and mission briefing
description: Title screen on load, difficulty level selection (1-10), then mission briefing showing fleet size and base count: YOUR MISSION: DESTROY THE FLEET OF N JOVIAN SHIPS BEFORE THEY DESTROY THE M UNITED PLANET SYSTEM BASES. GOOD LUCK! Press any key to begin. Depends on #62.

summary: Space Warp: maser fire sound effect
description: Sound effect for maser beam firing. Requires sound/DAC library (issue #36). Future enhancement.

summary: Space Warp: triton missile launch sound
description: Sound effect for triton missile launch and travel. Requires sound/DAC library (issue #36). Future enhancement.
Sound effect for Jovian ship destruction and other explosions. Requires sound/DAC library (issue #36). Future enhancement.
Sound effect for docking at a UP base. Requires sound/DAC library (issue #36). Future enhancement.
Audio alarm when SOS-BASE alert is active. Requires sound/DAC library (issue #36). Future enhancement.
Dramatic countdown sound/tone sequence during self-destruct confirmation. Requires sound/DAC library (issue #36). Future enhancement.

Randomly toggle star pixel patterns between artifact colors each frame for a living starfield effect. Stars shift between blue/red/white bit pairs. Subtle visual polish. Future enhancement. Depends on #62.
Persist high scores across game restarts, similar to Tetris implementation. Display top 5 scores with difficulty level. Show on game over screen. Future enhancement. Depends on #81.
